description: Create a new appliance operator tool under ee/appliance instead of extending the existing Nushell developer CLI.
Separate the appliance operator into an interactive TUI shell and a reusable non-interactive operator core.
Implement appliance environment discovery that can resolve the active site config, talosconfig, kubeconfig, and release manifests without requiring the operator to type those paths.
Structure path and asset resolution so the operator core can support both repo-based use and future standalone packaging.
Add a terminal home screen with primary actions for Bootstrap, Upgrade, Reset, and Status.
Show the selected appliance, node IP, release version, and high-level connectivity state in a persistent header or summary area.
Implement a guided bootstrap flow that gathers node IP, hostname, app URL, release version, bootstrap mode, and network settings through forms instead of raw flags.
Drive bootstrap through the existing bootstrap-appliance.sh script rather than reimplementing Talos or Flux bootstrap logic.
Display bootstrap progress as readable phases and streamed output instead of exposing raw shell commands as the primary UX.
Classify bootstrap failures into host, Kubernetes, Flux, Helm, or workload layers for operator guidance.
Implement an upgrade flow that shows the current installed appliance release and lets the operator select a published target release version.
Drive upgrades through the existing upgrade-appliance.sh behavior and release manifest model instead of asking for raw component tags.
Make the upgrade flow explicitly reflect the no-auto-rollback policy and point to support-bundle collection on failure.
Implement a separate reset flow with strong destructive confirmation before invoking reset-appliance-data.sh.
State exactly what reset wipes before allowing the operator to continue.
Implement a status dashboard that summarizes Talos reachability and key host health without showing raw talosctl commands.
Include Kubernetes node readiness and basic cluster availability in the status dashboard.
Include Flux GitRepository, Kustomization, and appliance HelmRelease readiness in the status dashboard.
Include PSA workload status for alga-core, db, redis, pgbouncer, temporal, workflow-worker, email-service, and temporal-worker in the status dashboard.
Show the selected appliance release, release metadata, app URL, and resolved config paths in the status experience.
Normalize host, cluster, Flux, and workload data into one internal appliance status model for the UI and command outputs.
Handle partial-failure states cleanly, including Talos-only availability, Kubernetes down, Flux degraded, or workload unhealthy while the cluster remains reachable.
Provide a non-interactive appliance command surface that mirrors the interactive flows for automation and scripted support use.
Keep direct kubectl and talosctl usage hidden for standard operator flows while preserving support for advanced fallback use outside the TUI.
Update appliance documentation so the new operator TUI is the preferred user-facing workflow and the shell scripts are documented as lower-level internals or fallbacks.
Replace the current readline-based interactive shell with an Ink-based full-screen application shell while preserving the existing operator core.
Implement a persistent full-screen layout with dedicated header, action navigation, main content, and progress/help regions.
Convert bootstrap, upgrade, and reset from sequential prompts to keyboard-navigable form and confirmation panels inside the Ink UI.
Render long-running lifecycle progress inside a dedicated live panel that preserves surrounding UI context while scripts stream output.
Present status as an always-available dashboard panel within the Ink UI rather than only as printed report sections.
Keep the non-interactive appliance commands and the operator core intact while swapping only the interactive UI layer to Ink.
Add a top-level Workloads operation in the Ink UI for appliance-relevant pod inspection without sending operators to raw kubectl.
Restrict the workload console to appliance-relevant namespaces and pods by default, including msp, alga-system, and flux-system components needed for appliance operation.
Present a refreshable workload list that shows pod name, namespace, phase/status, ready containers, restart count, and age/uptime.
Auto-refresh the workload list on a short interval while preserving the current selection and current view state.
Add a full-screen pod log viewer that opens from the workload list and restores the prior workload layout when the operator presses Escape.
Implement bounded-memory log loading using chunked older-log retrieval so operators can scroll back through long logs without unbounded in-memory accumulation.
Support live log follow while the operator is at the bottom of the log stream and pause live-follow when the operator scrolls away from the bottom.
Add keyboard navigation for the workload list and log viewer, including pod selection, vertical scrolling, loading older logs near the top, and Escape-to-return behavior.
Introduce a workload/log adapter layer over kubectl that supplies appliance-scoped pod inventory and pod logs to the TUI without exposing raw kubectl usage to the operator.
